There's a couple things in here for you. I'm not going to spend time on it, but there's a couple things about Starbucks, because in reality Starbucks is about creating a place. And in a sense is a cult. There's status to being somebody who goes to Starbucks versus somebody who doesn't go to Starbucks. There's a very clear physical environment. There's an emotional environment. I find it fascinating their description of their own business, which they now describe as has the third place. So they don't think of themselves in the coffee or coffee shop business. They think of themselves as being in the third place business. Work home Starbucks. And that's why people go there. Well, clearly they don't go there for the coffee. You can get coffee anywhere. I mean, it's one of the most commoditized commodities of all. So they're there for other reasons, right? They're there because it's a social environment. They're there because it's the illusion of a social environment. They're there because their status to being there. It's the cool place to go. And so there's a very insider, outsider thing to their business that Denny's, for example, does not have, right? Condonuts does not have, right? And so consequently, you can get $4 for something that could cost 50 cents, which is sort of a side benefit of all of this is the better at this we are, the less price matters for anything that we do. And we'd all like to be in the position of not having to worry about price. So page 106, an exercise for you, and you can all go help each other, is describe the place you've constructed for those you want to influence. How do they perceive? How do you want them to perceive what's on your side of the wall? What is it like being over there? How is it different from being over here? How do you make it feel like a physical environment to them, even though it is in fact not a physical environment? It is a book they are reading. It is a course they are taking. It is an email they are getting three times a week, et cetera. How are you creating place in your business? Let's do 15 minutes and to the tables. It strikes me that it is getting more. Anybody? That's like the headline of his last ad. I have a farm boy in my ass. I've been snuckering you all this time. I'm really... I have a question about the place. What do you want to do and how much of it is your customer wants to know? Well, they know they can't enunciate. Two different things. It's about both. Obviously, whatever you do has to serve your purposes. If there are certain things you are unwilling to do, you have a right to your lifestyle preferences, your ethical concerns on and on. But within all of those parameters, the goal is to create as appealing an environment as we can for the people who we are attempting to influence, motivate, attract, collect money from. You have to know as much as you possibly can. We're getting to profiling before we finish today. But you have to know as much as you possibly can about that person and then be constructing exactly what they really deeply, emotionally and psychologically want to the best of your detecting ability as well as their stated preferences. Yeah. I'm not sure if you've misunderstood the exercise somewhat. I really looked at it more from perspective of not so much what does the customer want, but what does the customer perceive the place to be? And then use that as the beginning point of is that how you want your place to be perceived and if not, then try to adjust that. You're coming from both ends, right? You're coming from your side, you're coming from their side and you're trying to build something that suits your preferences and that obviously you're able and willing to fulfill, but that then is as appealing as possible to the psyche of the person you're trying to attract. If you take the Martha Stewart example, I mean she's probably very pleased with the way her place has perceived it to be today. So now it's just a case of if you look at your place and say gee, is it being perceived properly then you've got your place. Yeah and see an interesting thing about Martha is that for example we've seen in the past year and I'm sure she's thrilled, she knows what she had to do to renegotiate the contract, but while she still has product at Kmart, she now has product at Macy's and Macy's has really become a celebrity driven multi product line store which they weren't even two or three years ago. But my guess is the Macy's customer is a better customer for her than the Kmart customer. I thought that was sort of badly connected to start with and that her entire environment and persona and everything that she presents is more appealing to a Macy's customer than it is to a Kmart customer. You know the like us, the mid-sized company, the Inc guy for the most part is not our guy. So some of our guys have made it to the Inc environment but pulling from the Inc environment then had a real good match with us. And so if we wanted to go there we'd have to make some message changes, philosophy changes, environmental changes to really appeal to them and we'd have to decide whether we wanted to do that or not or run two parallel businesses or three parallel businesses or four parallel businesses.
